name: Remember
cognitiveDemand: Retrieve specific facts, terms, and procedures from memory. No transformation of the information is required — the learner reproduces what was presented.
exampleObjective: Given a list of 20 common ID abbreviations, the learner will correctly identify the full term each abbreviation represents with at least 90% accuracy.
exampleAssessment: Multiple-choice questions asking learners to identify correct definitions; fill-in-the-blank requiring exact terminology; matching exercises pairing terms to descriptions.
exampleActivity: Flashcard review using a spaced repetition tool; terminology-matching drag-and-drop exercise; open-book glossary quiz to build familiarity before mastery.
designNote: Remember-level training is appropriate for safety codes, regulatory terms, and product specifications that must be retrievable verbatim. It is rarely the right endpoint for most workplace learning — verify that the job task doesn't actually require Apply or higher.

name: Understand
cognitiveDemand: Construct meaning from instructional content by interpreting, classifying, or explaining information in the learner's own words. The learner demonstrates comprehension, not just recognition.
exampleObjective: After completing the module, the learner will explain the difference between formative and summative evaluation in plain language, without referring to course materials.
exampleAssessment: Short written or verbal explanation in the learner's own words; multiple-choice questions requiring interpretation rather than recall; concept map demonstrating how ideas connect.
exampleActivity: Jigsaw activity where learners teach each other; 'Explain it to a colleague' reflection prompt; discussion question requiring paraphrase; concept map completion from memory.
designNote: Understand-level learning is appropriate for onboarding content, policy awareness, and conceptual foundations. Like Remember, it is often a stepping stone — most job performance requires Apply or higher. If learners only need to explain a concept but never use it, question whether training is the right intervention.

name: Apply
cognitiveDemand: Use a learned procedure, concept, or principle to carry out a task or solve a problem in a new but familiar context. The learner executes, not just understands.
exampleObjective: Given a customer complaint scenario, the learner will demonstrate the four-step de-escalation protocol in the correct sequence, achieving a simulated resolution within 3 minutes.
exampleAssessment: Branching scenario where learners must select and execute the correct procedure; performance observation checklist completed by a supervisor; simulation with scored decision points.
exampleActivity: Hands-on practice simulation; role-play exercise with observer feedback; step-by-step procedure walk-through using a real case; supervised job performance with coaching.
designNote: Apply is the most common target level for workplace skills training. If the job requires the learner to do something — run a process, complete a procedure, use a tool — Apply is your floor. Most compliance, technical skills, and customer-facing training should reach at least this level.

name: Analyze
cognitiveDemand: Break information into component parts, identify patterns and relationships, and determine how elements relate to one another and to the overall structure or purpose.
exampleObjective: Given a completed needs assessment report, the learner will analyze the data to distinguish between knowledge/skill gaps and systemic or process barriers, providing supporting evidence for each category.
exampleAssessment: Case study with a structured analysis rubric; comparative analysis task requiring learners to break down two competing approaches by defined criteria; written analysis submitted for review.
exampleActivity: Root cause analysis exercise with real data; structured debate on competing interpretations of a scenario; 'fishbone' diagram completion; annotated case study review with a peer.
designNote: Analyze-level training is appropriate for supervisors, coaches, consultants, and anyone whose role involves diagnosing problems, comparing options, or interpreting data. It requires authentic case material and enough complexity that pattern-finding is genuinely necessary — a single obvious answer makes it Apply, not Analyze.

name: Evaluate
cognitiveDemand: Make judgments based on explicit criteria and standards. Justify a position, recommend a course of action, critique work, or select among alternatives — and defend the rationale.
exampleObjective: Given two competing instructional design approaches and a client brief, the learner will evaluate each option against five design criteria and justify a written recommendation.
exampleAssessment: Recommendation memo with criteria-based justification; scored rubric applied to a peer's deliverable; position paper defending a design decision; structured argumentation exercise.
exampleActivity: Design critique exercise using a rubric; peer review with structured feedback protocol; 'red team' challenge where learners argue against their own proposals; consultant-style decision brief.
designNote: Evaluate-level training is appropriate for senior practitioners, managers, and professionals who must make consequential decisions or provide expert judgment. It requires that learners have already internalized the criteria they're applying — you cannot evaluate well without first knowing what 'good' looks like at the Apply and Analyze levels.

name: Create
cognitiveDemand: Combine elements together to form a new, coherent whole — a plan, design, product, or original work that did not previously exist. The highest level of cognitive complexity.
exampleObjective: Given a client brief and learner analysis findings, the learner will design a complete instructional blueprint including stated CLOs, a module outline, an assessment strategy, and a media plan.
exampleAssessment: Portfolio submission with original deliverable; instructional design project created from a novel brief; prototype or working draft evaluated against design criteria; capstone project with stakeholder review.
exampleActivity: Design challenge using a real-world brief; capstone curriculum project; build an original assessment for a given objective; develop a training plan for a workplace scenario you bring from your own role.
designNote: Create-level training is the domain of advanced professional development, graduate-level education, and high-complexity practitioner training. It requires learners to have deep fluency at Apply and Analyze first. For most organizational training programs, Create is the aspiration — the top of the journey, not the starting point.
